Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience

Negative Appraisals.value: I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
I can't stop bad things from happening to me
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I think about how life would have been different if the event had not occurred
Situations that make me feel strong emotions
I remind myself it could have been worse

Memory.value: When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.

vignette:
This 21-year-old Middle Eastern woman, who is divorced, presented with a persistent post-traumatic stress picture that has left her feeling changed in ways that dominate daily life. Her account centres on an interpersonal trauma, after which she describes a marked shift from a time when she “used to be a happy person” to one in which she now feels “always miserable.” She speaks with a strong sense that she cannot stop bad things from happening to her, and this sense of helplessness colours her attempts to move through ordinary situations. She also reports that she will never be able to feel normal emotions again, reflecting a deep loss of confidence in her own emotional life.

Her symptoms are easily activated by reminders that feel unpredictable and intrusive in everyday settings. Talking about snakes or seeing photos of them can set off a surge of distress, as can being forced to do something or having to talk to strangers. When reminders arise, she experiences intense fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame, together with strong physical reactions. Sleep is also disrupted, with difficulty falling asleep or staying asleep adding to her exhaustion and reducing her capacity to recover overnight.

When memories surface, they are not experienced as distant recollections. She sometimes hears sounds in her head that were present during the frightening event, and the memory can unfold like a film playing over and over, or feel as though it is happening again in the present moment. These experiences leave her more convinced that life has been permanently diminished. She spends time thinking about how things might have been different if the event had not occurred, while also telling herself it could have been worse, a brief attempt to manage the weight of what she feels.

She copes by avoiding situations that make her feel strong emotions, and she keeps some distance from unfamiliar social contact and from circumstances in which she may be compelled or cornered. Over time, this pattern has narrowed her life and made relationships more strained, while the ongoing threat state and disturbed sleep keep her on edge. The overall presentation is of a young woman caught between intrusive reliving, entrenched negative expectations about herself and the future, and coping efforts that offer temporary relief but preserve her distress.
